theorem: P ∨ Q, P → R, Q → R ⊢ R

steps.label: What we want to prove
steps.explanation: Given P ∨ Q and proofs that both P → R and Q → R, prove R. If we know one of P or Q holds, and both lead to R, then R must hold. This is proof by cases.

steps.label: Set up ∨-Elim (case split)
steps.explanation: ∨-Elimination requires three things: the disjunction P ∨ Q, a proof that P leads to R, and a proof that Q leads to R. We handle each case separately — if P holds, we get R one way; if Q holds, we get R another way.

steps.label: Complete both cases with →-Elim
steps.explanation: In Case 1, we have P and P → R, so →-Elim gives R. In Case 2, we have Q and Q → R, so →-Elim gives R. Both branches reach R, so the proof is complete.
